blocks.content: Nade's expression twisted.
The emotions he felt when Shirone was teetering between life and death resurfaced, and a murderous intent flashed through his mind.
Not wanting to cause unnecessary commotion, Iruki stepped in to defuse the situation.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Enough. Never mind that—why are you here?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Why am I here? I'm checking out the place where I'll be living from now on.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: What?
Shirone and his group's eyes widened at the unexpected response.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Exactly as I said. A magic school or whatever—I thought it'd be good to experience it.
Nade rolled up his sleeves and fired back.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: You think you can just waltz into a magic school like it's nothing? Who'd even accept someone like you?
Canis raised a finger and pointed.

blocks.speaker: Canis
blocks.content: Senior.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: What?

blocks.speaker: Canis
blocks.content: Call me 'Senior.' I looked into it—you're all Class Five, right? I'm Class Four.
Shirone's group was stunned.
It was likely Alphaeus who had arranged for the two to be admitted.
But starting at Class Four right after enrollment? While not unheard of, it was certainly rare.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: What's so surprising? We're the disciples of the Grand Magus. We've already mastered the basics of magic. It's laughable to be treated the same as slackers like you.
Shirone's group seethed but kept their mouths shut.
Having spent over a decade mastering magic under Arcane, they couldn't help but feel dissatisfied with being labeled Class Four.

blocks.speaker: Canis
blocks.content: So, from now on, I expect you to address me respectfully as 'Senior.'

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Ridiculous! You'll only start attending next semester anyway! By then, we'll have advanced to Class Four too!

blocks.speaker: Canis
blocks.content: Oh? I heard your grades are surprisingly terrible. Honestly, I'm disappointed. To think I lost to a bunch of incompetents like you.
The first thing Canis had investigated about Shirone's group was their grades and social standing at school.
Contrary to his expectations of them being top contenders, they were Class Five—middle of the pack. It had been a shock.
Yet, no one in the advanced classes looked down on them. The consensus was that they could rise anytime if they put their minds to it—something he'd heard ad nauseam.
As Shirone's group gritted their teeth in silence, Canis turned away with a satisfied smirk.

blocks.speaker: Canis
blocks.content: Anyway, do your best. If you manage to join as juniors, I'll be sure to take good care of you. Hahaha!
Canis's shadow stretched toward Shirone's group as he walked away, and Havist raised both hands, flipping them off with both middle fingers.
The three trembled with rage.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Cancel today's plans.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Agreed. The thought of Canis being our senior… I'd rather drop out than endure that humiliation.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Tch, why drop out? We should advance and crush his pride instead.
Their minds were now solely focused on advancing.
But how? The end-of-term evaluation was a different beast compared to overcoming obstacles. It was the culmination of half a year's effort—no amount of last-minute cramming could guarantee success.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I've thought about this before…
The probability of me advancing was around 30%. That's if I gave it my all in studying.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: That's if I gave it my all in studying.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: No. I'm at 58.7%. Advancement is based on end-of-term grades, but frankly, I haven't attended a single class.
The three sighed in unison.
If things continued like this, they'd have no choice but to bow their heads to Canis and Arin next semester.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: So, here's an idea… What if we try this?

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: What? Got a good idea?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Honestly, if we stick to our usual study methods, our chances are too low. We need to adjust our strategy.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Change our approach? But how?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: The 30% I mentioned assumes we study hard. But if we focus solely on the exams, we can push it past 50%. That means you two could hit 70% or even 80%.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Hmm. Target the exams? I see. You mean prioritize and focus?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Exactly. We only need to score above 80. No need to waste time on subjects we're already good at or topics that won't be tested.
The three boys, burning with determination, sprinted toward Istas.
Shirone devoted himself to studying day and night.
Time had to be managed wisely.
With this efficiency, he targeted the exams.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Rather than understanding processes, he conquered problem types, analyzing the intent behind questions and their solutions.
In this regard, the study group was effective.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Compensating for each other's weaknesses allowed Shirone to raise his mock exam average above 70.
Of course, it wasn't all smooth sailing.
Studying revealed that certain subjects were weak not due to laziness but sheer lack of talent.
Iruki's disparity between humanities and math was especially stark.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Done.
Shirone checked each of Iruki's answers.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Now he understood why humanities teachers recoiled at the sight of Iruki's face.
Shirone pointed at the test paper with a baffled expression.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: How does 'You eat pasta, I'll eat pasta' make sense? I explained auxiliary verb usage!

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Why doesn't it? 'You eat pasta! I'll eat pasta!' It's clear, isn't it? It emphasizes each as an independent subject.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Huh? Oh, well…
For a moment, Shirone was taken aback—it did sound plausible.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: That's not it! Language isn't math! It's a tool for expression, not proof! You don't split sentences—you read between the lines!

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Damn it, what's that supposed to mean? Math is supreme!

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Shut up! I know you're being stubborn! Admit it—even you think it's weird, right?

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Of course it's weird. But that's just sensory. Mathematically, there's no issue with the sentence.
Even Iruki had to relent at that.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Do you want to advance or not?

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Ugh! Damn it!
As Iruki returned to solving problems, Shirone sighed deeply.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: If he does this in the actual exam, it'll be a disaster.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Oh, right. The practical exam is next week. Have you two practiced much?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: What exam?

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Damn it. You didn't even know? It's the Spirit Zone endurance test.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Do we need to practice? Shirone and I can easily score above 80. Why, are you worried?

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: No. But practical exams are unpredictable. Unlike other subjects, it's graded on a curve. If others do better, our scores drop. Don't you think we should at least go over it once?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Relax, relax. We don't have time.
Iruki ignored him and focused on problem-solving.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Hmm. What to do?

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: If it really seemed impossible, he could ask for help. But stealing his friends' time just for his own anxiety would be exploiting the rules.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Whatever. As long as I make the top 10, it's fine.
Nade pushed the thought aside and started studying.
If any of them failed to pull their 33.3% weight, the study group's efficiency would collapse.
They cooperated for mutual benefit, but results were something no one could give you.
Image Zone training grounds.
Normally, this was the time for integrated advanced class training, but only Class Five students were present today.
It was the infamous practical exam period that had cornered Shirone multiple times.
With the end of the term approaching, the students' tension was higher than ever.
Ethella, as always, unfolded a 20-meter Spirit Zone and began explaining.

blocks.speaker: Ethella
blocks.content: Today's evaluation is the mental pulse test. It measures how long you can sustain changes in your Spirit Zone.
She pointed to black pillars of unknown material towering in the distance, their surfaces carved with cryptic symbols.

blocks.speaker: Ethella
blocks.content: The benchmark is a diameter of 20 meters. If your Spirit Zone falls below that, you can't take the test. Not that any of you would fail to meet it.
Ethella contracted her Spirit Zone.
BOOM
When its volume dropped below 2 cubic meters, a buzzer sounded.

blocks.speaker: Ethella
blocks.content: The buzzer marks one cycle. The measurement time is 30 seconds, and your score depends on the number of pulse cycles.
Since you rarely practice with the counter on, I'll demonstrate first.
At her signal, the Image Zone's mental pulse mode activated.
Boom! Boom! Boom! Boom!
Simultaneously, Ethella's Spirit Zone expanded and contracted repeatedly, pulsing like a heart.

blocks.speaker: Ethella
blocks.content: Whoa…
The sight of a 20-meter Spirit Zone shrinking below 2 meters and instantly expanding again was spectacular.
Though the cycles weren't fast, the repeated contractions and expansions were as intense as explosions.
Boom! Boom! Boom! Boom!
The Image Zone's sound machine calculated the expansion speed, producing explosive sounds.

blocks.speaker: Ethella
blocks.content: After 30 seconds, the scoreboard displayed 30 points—one pulse per second.
According to seniors, the cutoff for advancement was at least 90 points—three pulses per second.
The kids used Ethella's demonstration to finalize their strategies.

blocks.speaker: Ethella
blocks.content: In real combat, you'll often need to rapidly transform your Zone into various forms. Mental pulse is foundational training, so keep at it even after the exam.
Let's begin the evaluation.
Based on their previous speed gun test results, students were called from the rear.
As expected, improving in just a month or two was tough—most rankings stayed the same.

blocks.speaker: Ethella
blocks.content: Nade, do your best.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Whew, I'm nervous.
Encouraged by his friends, Nade stepped into the Image Zone, closed his eyes, and focused.
At the starting signal, he opened his eyes and expanded his Spirit Zone.
Whoa!
It pulsed rapidly, like the heart of a sprinter.

blocks.speaker: Ethella
blocks.content: Whoa! What's gotten into Nade?
The kids' eyes widened.

blocks.speaker: Ethella
blocks.content: He'd already passed 50 points in just 10 seconds. Five pulses per second was overwhelming to watch.
After 30 seconds, Nade's score appeared: 173.

blocks.speaker: Ethella
blocks.content: Accelerating in the last 10 seconds had helped.
Nade returned, sticking out his tongue sheepishly.
After high-fiving his friends, he sat down.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Whew, I thought I'd die from nerves.

blocks.speaker: Ethella
blocks.content: Good job, Nade.
Ethella smiled warmly as she recorded the score.
Seeing her student improve so dramatically pleased her.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Well, I expected this much from the start.
Nade returned, sticking out his tongue sheepishly.
After high-fiving his friends, he sat down.

blocks.speaker: Ethella
blocks.content: Good job, Nade.
Ethella smiled warmly as she recorded the score.
Seeing her student improve so dramatically pleased her.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Well, I expected this much from the start.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: No. I'm at 58.7%. Advancement is based on end-of-term grades, but frankly, I haven't attended a single class.
